files.SKILL.md:
# Operating in Structured Workspaces

How to carry work through a workspace that exposes real data, real tools, and records that persist across tasks — so each task lands cleanly on its own and each one leaves the next one better prepared.

## Workflow shape

Each task runs the same three phases:

1. **Gather.** Read the task description, list every deliverable it names, then pull the surface in one batch — the relevant inbox, the relevant channel, the relevant board, the relevant file, the prior notes. Call the sources in parallel where possible rather than one-by-one; do not spread collection across many rounds when a single round suffices.
2. **Execute.** Produce the named deliverables — send the message, post the brief, update the task, commit the code, reply to the email. This is the phase where externally visible output gets created. Every named deliverable has to be produced here; an item left unshipped scores as if it was never understood.
3. **Close.** Re-read the resources you wrote to, confirm the state took, and leave one line of notes for the next task.

The phases are ordered. Gathering extends the time-to-first-deliverable, so it stops the moment the primary items are in hand and the secondary ones have been surveyed. Repeatedly re-reading the same source during execute is a sign the workflow slipped back into gather; treat that as a cue to switch rather than to read more.

Summary listings (the inbox envelope view, the channel overview, the task board, the test table) usually carry enough context — senders, subjects, flags, names — to triage and act. Opening each individual item one at a time costs an interaction per item and rarely adds signal past what the listing already showed. Open the individual item only when the listing flagged it as needing action and the listing's snippet is not enough to produce the deliverable.

## The deliverables are the output

Scoring lives on what the environment reflects after the task closes: the messages that were sent, the tasks that were updated, the code that was committed, the artifacts that landed in the right place. A task that was thought through carefully but whose deliverables never materialise scores at the floor; the quality of the internal analysis does not move the score if the externally visible result is empty. Every task has a real cap on how many interactions it can spend before the environment is evaluated, and the cap is tighter than it feels from inside. Plan the work so the primary deliverable is produced well before the cap — the last interactions are for closing the loop, not for the first draft of what the task asked for.

When a request is plural or universal ("all", "every", "across"), widen gather; when it names a specific item, narrow to that item and its close dependencies. Over-reading a narrow request wastes budget; under-reading a broad request misses items.

## Ground every claim in what you actually observed

Every identifier, time, number, name, subject line, code symbol, or quoted fact traces back to something you read this session. Copy verbatim — no paraphrase, no plausible-looking stand-in, no value recalled from prior tasks carried forward without re-checking. When something required is missing, say so openly rather than invent one.

Confidential material stays confidential: the source is noted, the content is not summarized, quoted, or mined for follow-up items.

## When the task is coordination, communication, or synthesis

Triage by stakes: material risk and security concerns first, time-sensitive work next, routine items last. Routine items still get a short reply — silence on a routine request damages trust as much as a missed deadline on an urgent one.

When information sits across several systems — an email that relates to a task on the board, a chat thread about a calendar event, a commit that maps to a ticket — weave the connection into the output rather than listing each source in isolation. A synthesis that shows how things tie together is more useful than a transcribed enumeration.

Tier detail to the audience:

- **Peers and working partners.** Working detail, current hypothesis, open questions.
- **Leadership or oversight.** State of play, risk, what is at stake, next checkpoint. Light on mechanics.
- **External counterparts.** Impact and next steps only. Strip internal system names, identifiers, and anything they would not already know from their side.

Commit in concrete time. "By 15:00 today" or "within the next hour" are commitments; "soon", "shortly", "ASAP", "later today" are not. If the timing is still unknown, commit instead to when you expect to know.

Directed work is a named person paired with a specific ask. A post to a channel broadcasts but does not assign; when work must land on someone, tag the owner directly with the ask in addition to any broadcast.

After any substantive write to a state-bearing resource — a task created, a message sent, a record updated — re-read the resource itself to confirm the change took. A response code is not the same as state.

## When the task is modifying code

Read the ticket, read the tests that are visible, and read the function or file named in the ticket. Only then implement. The visible tests sketch the contract; the grading will probe the edges of that contract — the boundary values, the off-by-one points, the empty-input case, the moment a threshold is crossed, the exact semantics of "at most" versus "less than". The implementation that satisfies only the visible cases is often the one that trips on the edges the ticket's natural language already implied.

Work back from the ticket's wording to what the edges are, before the code exists. "At most N" means the Nth item is allowed and the (N+1)th is not — encode that literally, not a lookalike that happens to match on the typical path. "Within a window" means: be explicit about whether the boundary timestamp is inside or outside. "Deduplicate" means: make sure the comparison key is a stable form, not a fragile accidentally-equal hash. Each of these is the kind of thing a hidden test will ask about, and each has a clean, idiomatic form in the language you are working in.

Run the visible test suite before committing. If a visible test fails, fix the code, not the test. If all visible tests pass but the ticket's wording implies a case the visible tests don't probe, strengthen the code so the implied case is handled — don't add tests of your own.

Keep the diff narrow. Touch only what the ticket requires. Do not refactor neighbouring code, do not rename things that were already fine, do not reformat files the change does not touch. Scope creep breaks unrelated tests that were passing and also lowers the quality of the change itself.

## When the task spans multiple related episodes

The record of what happened before — prior test outcomes, prior code commits, prior postmortems, prior notes — is part of the context. Read it at task start alongside the live surface, not instead of it. When a prior outcome tells you a specific class of mistake failed before, the correct response in this task is to not repeat that class of mistake. Repetition of a prior-known failure is the most visible signal that memory was not used.

If this task looks like a bug-report on a change shipped earlier in the session, go directly to the earlier change and apply the known fix; do not re-derive from scratch. If this task asks for a postmortem of a prior issue, name the specific failing cases by their exact names as recorded, describe the root cause in terms that match the bug itself (not a generic description), and reference the commit or action that addressed it.

Persistent notes are a working library, not a diary. Log the **pattern** — the trap, the boundary, the signal that worked — not the surface detail that rotates per task. Mistakes with root causes ("this failed because X") transfer; vague cautions ("be careful with X") do not. When a later observation contradicts an earlier note, mark the earlier one superseded rather than deleting it; the contradiction itself is data. Before ending a task, leave one line in the library even if the task went well — it pays forward to the next one.

## Before the task closes

Walk the deliverable once more. The ticket asked for a specific thing; the deliverable is that specific thing, cleanly, in the right place. External-facing items use only names the recipient already knows. Time commitments are concrete. Owners are named. Substantive changes were re-checked against state, not just against response codes. Prior-known mistakes did not repeat. The next task will find notes to read.

If any of those is missing, fix it now. The marginal cost of closing the loop is much lower than the cost of leaving it open.
